Tony tells the farmer story about wild horses and the plow. So he truly thinks like that. Yeah. Great. Okay. So let's move to module five then which is the third part of this intervention. And I wanted to talk about a strategy that Tony used during this entire three-part, the round table series called role shifting. So when you're working with someone and they seem stuck in terms of the emotional resources they can bring to the table, this is a great strategy. And what it means is that when someone is stuck struggling with a problem for a long time and this is a human tendency, we tend to build habits around that problem. So once we've had X amount of weeks or months dealing with a problem and it doesn't seem to be changing, we start habituating to it and building habits about it and sometimes even build an identity around that problem. So role shifting comes from the hypnotic tradition of Milton Erickson but Tony developed a conversational version without the person having to go into a hypnotic trance. It's just conversational. Of course the strategic tradition also had different forms of shifting roles for people. Now back to Joaquin. You know he had a story where through hard work and discipline he overcame difficult circumstances and built a successful career. But when that story was interrupted and he no longer had that success, he no longer felt he had the things going for him that had made him successful and he tried this, he tried that, he tried basketball camps but he became fatigued. And when people become fatigued by struggling with a problem, they no longer have the creativity and energy that's needed to come up with new resources, new scenarios and they become kind of locked into like being exhausted and just opposing something. Opposing something or opposing somebody. So Joaquin after a lifetime of taking responsibility and initiative is now stuck in this disempowering story and in opposition to his team and the habit of blaming and feeling that the world is against him. And this is of course, like we said, it doesn't mean that he wasn't betrayed, maybe there was something wrong that the team did. But certainly he's leaning on the story in a way that's depriving him of the self-ownership that we just talked about and was preventing him from having the resourcefulness and creativity initiative that he needed to have. So working with a client and they seem like they've been locked into that position for a while when they've gotten helpless about certain aspects and maybe you're even using the problem to meet their needs in some way, you need a strategy for bringing new emotional resources to the table. And so in Joaquin's case, Tony was looking for Joaquin's internal, his personal standards, his internal standards, his pride, discipline and humility and the things that had helped him to succeed before. And he was looking for that line that he could draw on the sand. So he can say, you know what, I don't go to self pity anymore. I don't go to helplessness anymore. This is who I am. This is who I am from now on. I may have been weak in the past. I'm not weak again. I'm never going back there. Now in role shifting, you can ask a person directly, but what you can do is get a person out of their own perspective where they've been stuck and isolated and help them to take on a new perspective and a new position and a new role where they can perceive themselves and their situation from a different position and in that way they get unlocked. And so it's pretty basic and you'd be amazed at how many clients will just willing to do this immediately. All you have to do is, listen, I want you to pretend that you're this person now and I'm going to ask you some questions. So for instance, when Tony noticed that Joaquin idolized Magic Johnson and played against him, he asked him to take Magic Johnson's perspective about Joaquin. And so now he's magic talking about Joaquin about what he wants and this kind of ejects Joaquin out of his own perspective so he has to take someone else's perspective and see it from that point of view. Now Joaquin is a tough client in the sense that he seems to be kind of closed. He's private. He plays his cards to his vest. He doesn't share openly what he's thinking, not feeling. He's a little bit like an underdog who says, you can misunderstand me or underestimate me now but I'm going to work hard and come back and succeed. So Tony has to be very careful not to criticize Joaquin in a way that he would feel that is unjust or that he would feel alienated. So Tony used another form of role shifting. The next version was when he asked a client about someone in their own life. And in this case, Tony wanted to know who was the formative influence on Joaquin's life. And so he asked him, whose love did you crave in your life? And Joaquin said his mother and thought of different family members and then he realized it was his grandmother. And so Tony asked, who did you have to be for your grandmother? This is an important question because all of our formative influences, they have requirements of us that we kind of have to conform to the rules as when we're children. And so Joaquin replied that he had to be positive, disciplined and ambitious and he had to have goals. So Tony pointed out to Joaquin that a large part of his model of the world was formed by his grandmother. And at that point Joaquin broke down crying and saying that his grandmother would not be proud of the way he was responding to the challenge right now. So this is a turning point in this session because the relationships are important. Many of us are more motivated by relationships than we would ever admit. And if you can bring a relationship into the coaching session as a source of emotional energy and understanding where someone's emotional anchors are, it can be very powerful. And so once Joaquin realized that he was falling short of the standards that his grandmother had taught him, after Tony had had difficult finding Joaquin's own standards for himself, he found his grandmother's standards. And that was the perspective that enabled Joaquin to see himself in another way. And so Tony had been trying to find the source for Joaquin's standards, but it wasn't until he found the grandmother's that he really found the line in the sand. Now since at that point Tony had made contact with Joaquin's kind of primal emotion about who he is and what he stands for, he follows us up with another role-shifting strategy. So he asks, for your grandmother, there are parts of you that have been asleep. Which parts of you have been feeling self-pity? And Joaquin said, just pity. And Tony says, what do you call that? Pity what? Pity boy? Which part of you has been running the show? And Joaquin says, pity little girl. Now for a man to be a little girl, to play like a girl in the culture of sports is kind of a common put down, right? And Tony had referred to it early in the session as well. Now this role-shifting strategy is about finding parts of yourself. I don't think Joaquin expected that he was actually going to be speaking like a girl and taking on that entire persona. But it's about finding parts of yourself and it's about finding parts of you that are active and the parts that are dormant. So it's about figuring out these parts of you and how they relate to each other and how they're trying to serve you by meeting your needs. So it's almost like all of us have a kind of team of different personalities or parts inside of us and they're just parts of me that are, you know, you can give them names and you can give them, you know, kind of characterize them. But they're really just parts of you that are trying to coordinate your energies and your attention and meet your needs so that you can respond well to situations. So the language that you use for this strategy is simple. And there's Tony's words. There's parts of you that some are active and some are sleep. Which part of you has been running the show? What do we call it? So Joaquin says, pity little girl. And then the question is, so where is the pity little girl? And you find it where in the person's body this part of the personality resides. So for Joaquin, the pity little girl is about knee-high just like a little child. And you ask, what is the sound of the pity little girl? And Joaquin made this whining, complaining noise. And then you say, I'm going to ask pity little girl some questions and I want you to answer as pity little girl. Pity little girl, what are you trying to give Joaquin? And the emphasis is that when you talk to a part of the person that you're talking to that part as if it was just the person inside Joaquin that's inevitably trying to serve and protect Joaquin. And what was interesting and fascinating was how pity little girl was able to give a completely different perspective on Joaquin than he himself had been able to give. So she said that she was trying to protect him from accomplishing his goals, trying to get people to feel sorry for him, that maybe they'll just give him something if they feel sorry enough so he doesn't have to work because what she really wants him for is to have an easy opportunity. And you could see how Joaquin was fascinated by hearing what pity little girl had to say and he really started getting the truth about where he'd been. So you may have noticed Tony tapping Joaquin on the hand. That's because that's where pity little girl lives on Joaquin and so Tony's tapping that as an anchor to emphasize when he's talking to pity little girl that he should respond as pity little girl and help strengthen the sense that it's a pity little girl that's speaking not Joaquin. And Tony talks to pity little girl as if she had all the control, not Joaquin. Tony asks pity little girl are you going to be willing to let another part of Joaquin run the show? And the point here is to understand the way that part was trying to help, explaining how it wasn't help, how it might have been hindrance, how they can help better by changing or letting go and letting another part be in control. So at this point Tony asked Joaquin where the real powerful part of himself lives and he says it's in his heart, he pointed to his heart. And then Tony asked where is that part of him now? And Joaquin says it's in Kim, it's in his wife. So Joaquin in his mind lives in Kim. That shows you how decentralized he was and how much Kim's strength and determination had been what was keeping the family together. So Tony now moves to another role shifting strategy which is what we call strategic archetypes. Tony developed this strategy on September 11, 2001 actually when he was working with a group of people who had just been traumatized by the terrorist attacks on that same day. And strategic archetypes is a way of tapping into four union archetypes inside the person, the warrior, the magician, the lover and the sovereign. And there's another way to bring out emotional resources and the way this works is by encouraging the person to really maximize one part of their personality at a time. So when Tony asks you to make the sound of the warrior, you're supposed to put all of your fighting spirit into that sound. So in Joaquin's case he was hitting his fist against his hand, maybe he's less vocal but it's also a pretty strong gesture. Some people will scream and yell when they're making the sound of the warrior. Now these four archetypes were designed to complement each other. So after the warrior and then Tony will ask Joaquin to answer questions of the warrior, the second one is the magician. Now the way that works is that you shift completely and you think of the funniest, trickiest, most common sense ways to look at a situation so it can be solved. So for example, what are all the resources that are out there? What are the things that we're overlooking? And so the third is the lover which is all about heartfelt feeling and connection and relationship and love and going beyond yourself. And then as the fourth is the sovereign which basically is like the king who takes feedback from the magician, the lover and the warrior and then prioritizes and decides what's most important. So this is an important strategy, role shifting. Tony's goal here in this module five was to really get Joaquin emotionally engaged and committed and bring all of his emotion to the surface and all of his emotional resources so he could accept the challenge in a way that was fully active, fully accepting and inviting the difficulty and then be able to use all of his creativity and initiative. And so he had to do this with a guy who had been strong and powerful and disciplined, who had become debilitated and withdrawn. And so by using this series of role shifting strategies, Tony's able to get this full emotional power to the surface and take him to the point where he's ready for a whatever challenge. I'll ask each one to write the story from the other person's perspective of what actually happened, as if it had happened, what the other person should. I like that too. What I find is that usually couples, even with the largest differences, they are pretty good at putting themselves into the other one's shoes.
